l	Z	d
d e#e
d!d"
d#d$
d$e%
Z&e&Z'd%d&
d'd(
Z)d)d*
Configuration functions for the logging package for Python. The core package
is based on PEP 282 and comments thereto in comp.lang.python, and influenced
by Apache's log4j system.
Copyright (C) 2001-2019 Vinay Sajip. All Rights Reserved.
To use, simply 'import logging' and log away!
ThreadingTCPServer
StreamRequestHandleriF#
    Read the logging configuration from a ConfigParser-format file.
    This can be called several times from an application, allowing an end user
    the ability to select from various pre-canned configurations (if the
    developer provides a mechanism to present the choices and load the chosen
    configuration).
    r
readline)

hand
section
klass
fmtr9
    When (re)configuring logging, handle loggers which were in the previous
    configuration but are not in the new configuration. There's no point
    deleting them as other threads may continue to hold references to them;
    and by disabling them, you stop them doing any logging.
    However, don't disable children of named loggers, as that's probably not
    what was intended by the user. Also, allow existing loggers to NOT be
    disabled if disable_existing is false.
    TN)
root
manager

PlaceHolderrA
NOTSETr

BaseConfigurator.cfg_convertc
        Convert values to an appropriate type. dicts, lists and tuples are
        replaced by their converting alternatives. Strings are checked to
        see if they have a conversion format and are converted if they do.

DictConfigurator.add_handlersFc
        Perform configuration which is common to root and non-root loggers.
        r<
common_logger_config
z%DictConfigurator.common_logger_configc
z.Configure a non-root logger from a dictionary.rP
z!DictConfigurator.configure_loggerc
z*Configure a root logger from a dictionary.N)
DictConfigurator.configure_rootN)
z%Configure logging using a dictionary.N)
dictConfigClassr
dictConfig'
    Start up a socket server on the specified port, and listen for new
    configurations.
    These will be sent as a file suitable for processing by fileConfig().
    Returns a Thread object on which you can call start() to start the server,
    and which you can join() when appropriate. To stop the server, call
    stopListening().
    Use the ``verify`` argument to verify any bytes received across the wire
    from a client. If specified, it should be a callable which receives a
    single argument - the bytes of configuration data received across the
    network - and it should return either ``None``, to indicate that the
    passed in bytes could not be verified and should be discarded, or a
    byte string which is then passed to the configuration machinery as
    normal. Note that you can return transformed bytes, e.g. by decrypting
    the bytes passed in.
    c
z#listen.<locals>.ConfigStreamHandlerz
        Handler for a logging configuration request.
        It expects a completely new logging configuration and uses fileConfig
        to install it.
        c
            Handle a request.
            Each request is expected to be a 4-byte length, packed using
            struct.pack(">L", n), followed by the config file.
            Uses fileConfig() to do the grunt work.
            
utf-8)
connectionZ
recvr1
structZ
unpack
server
verify
decode
json

	print_exc

)	z$listen.<locals>.ConfigSocketReceiverzD
        A simple TCP socket-based logging config receiver.
        rZ
